title: Toy Pirate Ship
summary: A tabletop pirate ship built from scrap 2x6s with angled bow cuts, dowel masts, canvas sails, and a candle-cup cannon or two.
description:
The whole hull shape comes from three progressively steeper angled cuts on stacked 2x6 scrap — roughly 20–25° for the bow point, then 10° and 15° for the deck steps behind it. There's no cut list with fixed numbers because there doesn't need to be one: stack your boards, cut the bow angle through all of them at once, then measure and mark the deck lengths to whatever scrap you actually have on hand. This is a genuinely good scrap-bin project for exactly that reason.

The one step worth planning ahead for is fastener placement — nail and glue the hull together with a clear mental map of where the mast holes are going, so you don't drive a nail straight into where you're about to drill. Canvas sails threaded on thin dowels and a candle-cup cannon or two finish it off; none of that needs to be precise, which is the point of a kid's toy.

tools.note: Set to angled bevel cuts for the bow and deck steps — no two cuts in this build are square.
3/4" bit for the mast holes, 1/4" for the sail-dowel holes.

materials.name: Pine, 2x6, scrap
materials.note: The whole hull — this is a good scrap-bin project.

materials.name: Dowel rod, 1/4"
materials.note: Hold the sails on the masts.

steps.title: Cut the bow point
steps.body: Start with the front of the ship. Set your circular saw to roughly a 20–25° angle and cut the point on three stacked boards at once, so all three come out matching.

steps.title: Cut the deck steps
steps.body: Set the saw to roughly 10° and cut the back end of the middle deck piece to your desired length (about 2.5 ft is typical). Set it on top of the bottom hull piece and mark where it needs to be cut to match. Cut the top two pieces at that same back angle, and at roughly 15° on their front edge.

steps.title: Cut the front deck piece and assemble the hull
steps.body: Keeping the same ~15° angle, cut the third front piece to your desired length (about 8" is typical). Nail and glue the whole hull together, keeping a clear mental map of where you'll be drilling mast holes next so you don't drive a nail into that spot.

steps.title: Drill masts, rig sails, and add cannons
steps.body: Drill 3/4" holes for the masts (18" dowel lengths work well) and 1/4" holes a couple inches from the top of each mast for the dowels that hold the sails. For a simple crow's nest, cut a small square from scrap 1x4, drill a 3/4" hole through it, and slide it onto a mast over the sail. Glue in the candle-cup cannons to finish.

images.alt: Toy wooden pirate ship with dowel masts, canvas sails, and candle-cup cannons
